message.content:
You are an independent reviewer on a cross-model panel. Answer the task below ENTIRELY ON YOUR OWN — you are one of three independent models; you cannot see the others. This is a pure reasoning/analysis task: do NOT explore any filesystem or codebase (you have no access to the repo in question); reason from the rich context given. Be concrete, adversarial, prioritized, and decisive. Return your full analysis as your final message (it will be judged and merged, not shown to a user directly).

=== TASK ===

Answer in ENGLISH. You are an independent reviewer pressure-testing a software build roadmap. Do NOT restate the plan — find what's wrong with it. Be concrete, adversarial, and prioritized.

CONTEXT: "AIGOS" generates a GTM strategy deck (6 positioning sections + a paid-media plan) from a company URL. Stack: Next.js 16, AI SDK v6, Supabase, Clerk, in-process "lab engine". We just PROVED (blind, on our own value-read rubric) that a GLM-5.2 agentic writer (tool-calling: web/reviews/keyword/ad APIs + a frozen research corpus) scores 8/8 on Voice-of-Customer + Demand-Intent, vs the current boxed-DeepSeek app's 3 and 1. A THIN deterministic "source-floor" (strip-or-gap, not 12 hard-fail verifiers) lifts floor-capped output to a clean 8/8. Architecture target = "agentic writer + ONE thin floor". Invariant: a section goes "absent" ONLY on schema/parse death, never on one bad number.

GOAL: reach a SUPERVISED PILOT — 2 internal operators run REAL client subjects through the app and trust the 7-section deck enough to use it in client (ad-budget) work. NOT self-serve. Model decision LOCKED: GLM-5.2 via Ollama now → provision a proper z.ai/BigModel GLM API key for prod.

THE PROPOSED PLAN (5 phases, 17-step critical path):
P0 Freeze the liar-catcher: SHA-256 byte-freeze the 12 existing verifier files as an "acknowledgement gate"; extend fabrication eval-fixtures to assert the fabrication is STRIPPED (today a fixture passes while the fabricated quote SURVIVES); per-guard regression-snapshot suite.
P1 Production GLM + output contract: provision z.ai GLM key + live-prove the 8/8 survives the Ollama→z.ai endpoint/model-id swap; LOCK that the agentic writer emits a TYPED body (structured-emit of GLM's own prose draft), not pure prose.
P2 core/ spine + thin floor + strangle the 14,971-line run-section.ts (run new spine in PARALLEL behind a flag, diff every commit-vs-absent decision + run verifier/floor on every new committed body): build source-floor.ts (unify existing strips + add ratio-recompute + quote-source-class), rewire committable-gate so a bad number → strip-and-commit not absent (WITHOUT re-enabling a quarantined "verdict→gapped" path that LAUNDERS fabrications), re-prove the floor on the RENDERED TYPED body ≥8.
P3 Extend to the other 5 sections (Market, BuyerICP, Competitor, Offer, PaidMedia) + cross-subject live validation on 3 DIVERSE non-Ramp subjects (everything so far is Ramp-only across 43+ runs), then retire the old path.
P4 Operator trust UX: per-section trust tiers, stop rerun/auto-rescue from silently destroying committed sections, prove the full browser flow E2E.

OUR OWN COMPLETENESS CRITIC ALREADY FOUND (go BEYOND these, don't repeat them): (1) FATAL circular dependency — P0.3's "assert post-strip deltas" needs the strip chain extracted as importable functions, but that extraction IS the P2 deliverable → "Phase 0 green" is unreachable as specced. (2) The value-read SCORER itself is unfrozen and can silently re-band every score. (3) The floor checks RATIOS but not raw LEAF numerics (a single CPC/search-volume) against the originating tool's RETURNED payload — the likeliest silent fabrication on a data-sparse subject. (4) z.ai parity must be the FIRST live action. (5) DeepSeek rollback is an availability fallback, NOT a quality one. (6) BuyerICP (needs a deterministic persona-grounding prepass) + PaidMedia (synthesis section) each need their own validation gate.

YOUR JOB (answer all five, concretely):
A. The SINGLE most likely reason this pilot fails in front of the 2 operators that NEITHER the plan NOR the critic named.
B. Is the phase SEQUENCING right? What would you re-order and why? Should P2's huge strangle precede or follow proving the agentic writer works cross-subject on all 7 sections? Is the strangler-don't-swap parallel-diff worth its cost for a 2-operator pilot, or over-engineering?
C. The deepest tension: a DETERMINISTIC strip-or-gap floor "never auto-corrects", but the proven-8 repair RECOMPUTED-and-showed a ratio (an LLM judgment). Can a purely deterministic floor hold 8/8, or does the floor itself need to be a small agentic/LLM pass? Argue both sides and pick one.
D. For a 2-operator supervised pilot specifically (not a product launch): what is OVER-built and should be cut to ship faster, and what is UNDER-built and will bite? Give the leanest critical path to "operators trust the deck on 3 real subjects".
E. Any blind spot in the whole framing — including whether "agentic writer + one thin floor" is even the right architecture vs alternatives.

attachment.content:
- ai-elements: AI Elements component library guidance — pre-built React components for AI interfaces built on shadcn/ui. Use when building chat UIs, message displays, tool call rendering, streaming responses, reasoning panels, or any AI-native interface with the AI SDK.
- ai-gateway: Vercel AI Gateway expert guidance. Use when configuring model routing, provider failover, cost tracking, or managing multiple AI providers through a unified API.
- ai-sdk: Vercel AI SDK expert guidance. Use when building AI-powered features — chat interfaces, text generation, structured output, tool calling, agents, MCP integration, streaming, embeddings, reranking, image generation, or working with any LLM provider.
- anura-design: Design system skill for anura. Activate when building UI components, pages, or any visual elements. Provides exact color tokens, typography scale, spacing grid, component patterns, and craft rules. Read references/DESIGN.md before writing any CSS or JSX.
- auto: Toggle Claude Code auto mode on/off. When enabled, Claude auto-approves safe
operations (file edits, bash commands, etc.) without prompting. Uses the built-in
auto mode classifier with allow/deny rules for safety.
Use when asked to "enable auto mode", "go auto", "stop asking me", or "auto approve".
- autoplan
- benchmark
- benchmark-models
- browse: Fast headless browser for QA testing and site dogfooding. Navigate any URL, interact with
elements, verify page state, diff before/after actions, take annotated screenshots, check
responsive layouts, test forms and uploads, handle dialogs, and assert element states.
~100ms per command. Use when you need to test a feature, verify a deployment, dogfood a
user flow, or file a bug with evidence. Use when asked to "open in browser", "test the
site", "take a screenshot", or "dogfood this". (gstack)
- canary
- careful
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by dropping filler, articles, and pleasantries while keeping full technical accuracy. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman.
- chat-sdk
- codex: OpenAI Codex CLI wrapper — three modes. Code review: independent diff review via
codex review with pass/fail gate. Challenge: adversarial mode that tries to break
your code. Consult: ask codex anything with session continuity for follow-ups.
The "200 IQ autistic developer" second opinion. Use when asked to "codex review",
"codex challenge", "ask codex", "second opinion", or "consult codex". (gstack)
Voice triggers (speech-to-text aliases): "code x", "code ex", "get another opinion".
- connect-chrome: Launch GStack Browser — AI-controlled Chromium with the sidebar extension baked in.
Opens a visible browser window where you can watch every action in real time.
The sidebar shows a live activity feed and chat. Anti-bot stealth built in.
Use when asked to "open gstack browser", "launch browser", "connect chrome",
"open chrome", "real browser", "launch chrome", "side panel", or "control my browser".
Voice triggers (speech-to-text aliases): "show me the browser".
- context-restore: Restore working context saved earlier by /context-save. Loads the most recent
saved state (across all branches by default) so you can pick up where you
left off — even across Conductor workspace handoffs.
Use when asked to "resume", "restore context", "where was I", or
"pick up where I left off". Pair with /context-save.
Formerly /checkpoint resume — renamed because Claude Code treats /checkpoint
as a native rewind alias in current environments. (gstack)
- context-save
- cso
- design: Unified frontend design workflow. Orchestrates Impeccable (18 audit sub-skills), SkillUI (reverse-engineer a site into a design system), Taste Skill (anti-slop taste guardrails, 8 flavors), Awesome Design MD (60+ brand DESIGN.md files), and UI UX Pro Max (styles, palettes, font pairings, stack patterns). All five are installed on this machine. Invoke on: design, build UI, create component, new page, update layout, style this, redesign, polish UI, make it look good, improve the design, frontend feature, hero section, landing page, dashboard, pricing page, reference a brand, clone Stripe, clone Vercel, anti-slop.
- design-consultation: Design consultation: understands your product, researches the landscape, proposes a
complete design system (aesthetic, typography, color, layout, spacing, motion), and
generates font+color preview pages. Creates DESIGN.md as your project's design source
of truth. For existing sites, use /plan-design-review to infer the system instead.
Use when asked to "design system", "brand guidelines", or "create DESIGN.md".
Proactively suggest when starting a new project's UI with no existing
design system or DESIGN.md. (gstack)
- design-html
- design-review: Designer's eye QA: finds visual inconsistency, spacing issues, hierarchy problems,
AI slop patterns, and slow interactions — then fixes them. Iteratively fixes issues
in source code, committing each fix atomically and re-verifying with before/after
screenshots. For plan-mode design review (before implementation), use /plan-design-review.
Use when asked to "audit the design", "visual QA", "check if it looks good", or "design polish".
Proactively suggest when the user mentions visual inconsistencies or
wants to polish the look of a live site. (gstack)
- design-shotgun
- devex-review
- diagnose
- document-release
- dream: Memory consolidation skill for Claude Code. Scans session transcripts for corrections, decisions, preferences, and patterns, then merges findings into persistent memory files. Auto-triggers via native Stop hook every 24hrs. Inspired by how sleep consolidates human memory.
- env-vars: Vercel environment variable expert guidance. Use when working with .env files, vercel env commands, OIDC tokens, or managing environment-specific configuration.
- freeze
- fusion: Answer a hard question by fanning it out to a PANEL of models running in parallel — each answering independently with web search and bash, none seeing the others' work — then having Opus 4.8 judge every response into a structured analysis (consensus, contradictions, partial coverage, unique insights, blind spots) and write a final answer grounded in it. The panel is two independent Opus 4.8 runs (slug opus4.8-4.8), Opus 4.8 + GPT-5.5 via codex (opus4.8-gpt5.5), or those plus GLM 5.2 via Ollama Cloud (opus4.8-gpt5.5-glm5.2). Opus always judges and writes the final answer — the pipeline can't be reversed. Runs on local CLI subscriptions (no metered API), saves a timestamped provenance .md per run, and answers in French by default. Use this whenever the user asks to "run it through Fusion", says /fusion, wants a multi-model / panel / ensemble answer, wants a question cross-checked across models, or wants a higher-confidence answer with consensus and blind spots surfaced — even if they don't say "fusion". General-purpose: any topic (research, law, strategy, technical, personal). Best for high-stakes research, design calls, and debugging where being confidently wrong is expensive.
- grill-with-docs: Interview the user relentlessly about a plan or design until reaching shared understanding, resolving each branch of the decision tree. Two modes: with-docs (challenges your plan against the existing domain model and updates CONTEXT.md / ADRs inline) and no-docs (just the interview loop when no domain docs are present). Use when user wants to stress-test a plan, get grilled on their design, or mentions "grill me" / "grill with docs".
- gstack
- gstack-upgrade: Upgrade gstack to the latest version. Detects global vs vendored install,
runs the upgrade, and shows what's new. Use when asked to "upgrade gstack",
"update gstack", or "get latest version".
Voice triggers (speech-to-text aliases): "upgrade the tools", "update the tools", "gee stack upgrade", "g stack upgrade".
- guard
- handoff: Compact the current conversation into a handoff document for another agent to pick up.
- health
- improve-codebase-architecture: Find deepening opportunities in a codebase, informed by the domain language in CONTEXT.md and the decisions in docs/adr/. Use when the user wants to improve architecture, find refactoring opportunities, consolidate tightly-coupled modules, or make a codebase more testable and AI-navigable.
- investigate: Systematic debugging with root cause investigation. Four phases: investigate,
analyze, hypothesize, implement. Iron Law: no fixes without root cause.
Use when asked to "debug this", "fix this bug", "why is this broken",
"investigate this error", or "root cause analysis".
Proactively invoke this skill (do NOT debug directly) when the user reports
errors, 500 errors, stack traces, unexpected behavior, "it was working
yesterday", or is troubleshooting why something stopped working. (gstack)
- land-and-deploy
- landing-report
- learn
- m2c1: Meta orchestration framework for autonomous software development. Use when building any software project from scratch or extending existing codebases. Converts brain dumps into phased implementation plans with parallel research, discovery questioning, tool setup, task sharding, synergy review, and multi-angle testing at every level. 12-phase workflow from idea to fully tested, deployed software.
- make-pdf: Turn any markdown file into a publication-quality PDF. Proper 1in margins,
intelligent page breaks, page numbers, cover pages, running headers, curly
quotes and em dashes, clickable TOC, diagonal DRAFT watermark. Not a draft
artifact — a finished artifact. Use when asked to "make a PDF", "export to
PDF", "turn this markdown into a PDF", or "generate a document". (gstack)
Voice triggers (speech-to-text aliases): "make this a pdf", "make it a pdf", "export to pdf", "turn this into a pdf", "turn this markdown into a pdf", "generate a pdf", "make a pdf from", "pdf this markdown".
- nextjs
- observability
- office-hours: YC Office Hours — two modes. Startup mode: six forcing questions that expose
demand reality, status quo, desperate specificity, narrowest wedge, observation,
and future-fit. Builder mode: design thinking brainstorming for side projects,
hackathons, learning, and open source. Saves a design doc.
Use when asked to "brainstorm this", "I have an idea", "help me think through
this", "office hours", or "is this worth building".
Proactively invoke this skill (do NOT answer directly) when the user describes
a new product idea, asks whether something is worth building, wants to think
through design decisions for something that doesn't exist yet, or is exploring
a concept before any code is written.
Use before /plan-ceo-review or /plan-eng-review. (gstack)
- pair-agent
- peon-ping-config: Update peon-ping configuration — volume, pack rotation, categories, active pack, and other settings. Use when user wants to change peon-ping settings like volume, enable round-robin, add packs to rotation, toggle sound categories, or adjust any config.
- peon-ping-toggle
- plan-ceo-review: CEO/founder-mode plan review. Rethink the problem, find the 10-star product,
challenge premises, expand scope when it creates a better product. Four modes:
SCOPE EXPANSION (dream big), SELECTIVE EXPANSION (hold scope + cherry-pick
expansions), HOLD SCOPE (maximum rigor), SCOPE REDUCTION (strip to essentials).
Use when asked to "think bigger", "expand scope", "strategy review", "rethink this",
or "is this ambitious enough".
Proactively suggest when the user is questioning scope or ambition of a plan,
or when the plan feels like it could be thinking bigger. (gstack)
- plan-design-review: Designer's eye plan review — interactive, like CEO and Eng review.
Rates each design dimension 0-10, explains what would make it a 10,
then fixes the plan to get there. Works in plan mode. For live site
visual audits, use /design-review. Use when asked to "review the design plan"
or "design critique".
Proactively suggest when the user has a plan with UI/UX components that
should be reviewed before implementation. (gstack)
- plan-devex-review
- plan-eng-review: Eng manager-mode plan review. Lock in the execution plan — architecture,
data flow, diagrams, edge cases, test coverage, performance. Walks through
issues interactively with opinionated recommendations. Use when asked to
"review the architecture", "engineering review", or "lock in the plan".
Proactively suggest when the user has a plan or design doc and is about to
start coding — to catch architecture issues before implementation. (gstack)
Voice triggers (speech-to-text aliases): "tech review", "technical review", "plan engineering review".
- plan-tune
- prototype: Build a throwaway prototype to flesh out a design before committing to it. Routes between two branches — a runnable terminal app for state/business-logic questions, or several radically different UI variations toggleable from one route. Use when the user wants to prototype, sanity-check a data model or state machine, mock up a UI, explore design options, or says "prototype this", "let me play with it", "try a few designs".
- qa: Systematically QA test a web application and fix bugs found. Runs QA testing,
then iteratively fixes bugs in source code, committing each fix atomically and
re-verifying. Use when asked to "qa", "QA", "test this site", "find bugs",
"test and fix", or "fix what's broken".
Proactively suggest when the user says a feature is ready for testing
or asks "does this work?". Three tiers: Quick (critical/high only),
Standard (+ medium), Exhaustive (+ cosmetic). Produces before/after health scores,
fix evidence, and a ship-readiness summary. For report-only mode, use /qa-only. (gstack)
Voice triggers (speech-to-text aliases): "quality check", "test the app", "run QA".
- qa-only
- react-best-practices
- retro
- review: Pre-landing PR review. Analyzes diff against the base branch for SQL safety, LLM trust
boundary violations, conditional side effects, and other structural issues. Use when
asked to "review this PR", "code review", "pre-landing review", or "check my diff".
Proactively suggest when the user is about to merge or land code changes. (gstack)
- routing-middleware
- scrape
- setup-browser-cookies: Import cookies from your real Chromium browser into the headless browse session.
Opens an interactive picker UI where you select which cookie domains to import.
Use before QA testing authenticated pages. Use when asked to "import cookies",
"login to the site", or "authenticate the browser". (gstack)
- setup-deploy
- setup-gbrain
- shadcn
- ship: Ship workflow: detect + merge base branch, run tests, review diff, bump VERSION,
update CHANGELOG, commit, push, create PR. Use when asked to "ship", "deploy",
"push to main", "create a PR", "merge and push", or "get it deployed".
Proactively invoke this skill (do NOT push/PR directly) when the user says code
is ready, asks about deploying, wants to push code up, or asks to create a PR. (gstack)
- skillify
- sync-gbrain
- tdd: Test-driven development with red-green-refactor loop. Use when user wants to build features or fix bugs using TDD, mentions "red-green-refactor", wants integration tests, or asks for test-first development.
- to-issues
- to-prd: Turn the current conversation context into a PRD and publish it to the project issue tracker. Use when user wants to create a PRD from the current context.
- triage
- ui-ux-pro-max: UI/UX design intelligence. 67 styles, 96 palettes, 57 font pairings, 25 charts, 13 stacks (React, Next.js, Vue, Svelte, SwiftUI, React Native, Flutter, Tailwind, shadcn/ui). Actions: plan, build, create, design, implement, review, fix, improve, optimize, enhance, refactor, check UI/UX code. Projects: website, landing page, dashboard, admin panel, e-commerce, SaaS, portfolio, blog, mobile app, .html, .tsx, .vue, .svelte. Elements: button, modal, navbar, sidebar, card, table, form, chart. Styles: glassmorphism, claymorphism, minimalism, brutalism, neumorphism, bento grid, dark mode, responsive, skeuomorphism, flat design. Topics: color palette, accessibility, animation, layout, typography, font pairing, spacing, hover, shadow, gradient. Integrations: shadcn/ui MCP for component search and examples.
- unfreeze
- vercel-cli
- vercel-functions
- verification
- video-use: Edit any video by conversation. Transcribe, cut, color grade, generate overlay animations, burn subtitles — for talking heads, montages, tutorials, travel, interviews. No presets, no menus. Ask questions, confirm the plan, execute, iterate, persist. Production-correctness rules are hard; everything else is artistic freedom.
- visual-plan: Turn ordinary text plans into rich interactive visual plans with diagrams, file maps, annotated code, open questions, and UI/prototype review when useful.
- write-a-skill
- dream: Memory consolidation skill for Claude Code. Scans session transcripts for corrections, decisions, preferences, and patterns, then merges findings into persistent memory files. Auto-triggers via native Stop hook every 24hrs. Inspired by how sleep consolidates human memory.
- playwright-cli: Automates browser interactions for web testing, form filling, screenshots, and data extraction. Use when the user needs to navigate websites, interact with web pages, fill forms, take screenshots, test web applications, or extract information from web pages.
- fusion-3: Fusion full panel — Opus 4.8 + GPT-5.5 + GLM 5.2 in parallel, judged by Opus 4.8 (opus4.8-gpt5.5-glm5.2)
- fusion-gpt5.5: Fusion panel of Opus 4.8 + GPT-5.5 in parallel, judged by Opus 4.8 (opus4.8-gpt5.5)
- fusion-opus4.8: Fusion panel of two independent Opus 4.8 runs, judged by Opus 4.8 (opus4.8-4.8)
- grade-scripts: Grade ad scripts like a world-class DR copywriter. Scores on 7 dimensions, audits for AI tells, suggests specific rewrites. Use with pasted scripts or point to a file/pack.
- gsd:add-phase: Add phase to end of current milestone in roadmap

- research-competitor: /research-competitor
- visual-plan: Create an interactive Agent-Native visual plan for the current task.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

- claude-md-management:revise-claude-md

- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.

- superpowers:brainstorming: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.
- superpowers:dispatching-parallel-agents: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies
- superpowers:executing-plans: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints
- superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch: Use when implementation is complete, all tests pass, and you need to decide how to integrate the work - guides completion of development work by presenting structured options for merge, PR, or cleanup
- superpowers:receiving-code-review
- superpowers:requesting-code-review: Use when completing tasks, implementing major features, or before merging to verify work meets requirements
- superpowers:subagent-driven-development: Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session
- superpowers:systematic-debugging: Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes
- superpowers:test-driven-development: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code
- superpowers:using-git-worktrees: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - ensures an isolated workspace exists via native tools or git worktree fallback
- superpowers:using-superpowers: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring skill invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
- superpowers:verification-before-completion: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always
- superpowers:writing-plans: Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code
- superpowers:writing-skills: Use when creating new skills, editing existing skills, or verifying skills work before deployment
- supabase:supabase: Use when doing ANY task involving Supabase. Triggers: Supabase products (Database, Auth, Edge Functions, Realtime, Storage, Vectors, Cron, Queues); client libraries and SSR integrations (supabase-js, @supabase/ssr) in Next.js, React, SvelteKit, Astro, Remix; auth issues (login, logout, sessions, JWT, cookies, getSession, getUser, getClaims, RLS); Supabase CLI or MCP server; schema changes, migrations, security audits, Postgres extensions (pg_graphql, pg_cron, pg_vector).

- chrome-devtools-mcp:chrome-devtools: Uses Chrome DevTools via MCP for efficient debugging, troubleshooting and browser automation. Use when debugging web pages, automating browser interactions, analyzing performance, or inspecting network requests. This skill does not apply to `--slim` mode (MCP configuration).

- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin

- claude-mem:smart-explore: Token-optimized structural code search using tree-sitter AST parsing. Use instead of reading full files when you need to understand code structure, find functions, or explore a codebase efficiently.

- impeccable:audit
- impeccable:bolder
- impeccable:clarify
- impeccable:colorize
- impeccable:critique
- impeccable:delight
- impeccable:distill
- impeccable:harden
- impeccable:impeccable
- impeccable:layout
- impeccable:optimize
- impeccable:overdrive
- impeccable:polish
- impeccable:quieter
- impeccable:shape
- impeccable:typeset
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review

content.text:
This is a pure reasoning task. I'll answer all five parts directly, going beyond the named issues. No tools needed.

---

# Pressure Test: AIGOS Agentic-Writer Pilot Roadmap

## A. The single most likely reason the pilot fails — unnamed by plan or critic

**The pilot fails because the operators stop trusting the deck after they catch ONE confident, well-sourced, plausible-but-wrong *qualitative* claim — and your entire defense apparatus only guards numbers.**

Every gate you've built or planned — source-floor, ratio-recompute, quote-source-class, leaf-numeric checks, the 12 verifiers — operates on *checkable atoms*: numbers, quotes, URLs. But the unit of operator trust is not the atom; it's the **strategic assertion**. The deck's value lives in claims like "Ramp's wedge is bottom-up finance-team adoption that bypasses procurement," "the buyer's dominant anxiety is audit-readiness, not cost," "the competitor is vulnerable on mid-market because their pricing punishes seat growth." These are exactly what a GLM-5.2 agentic writer scoring 8/8 on a *value-read rubric* is good at producing — fluent, specific, synthesis-grade prose. And **none of them are gated by anything you've built or plan to build.** A strip-or-gap floor cannot strip "their wedge is bottom-up adoption" — there's no leaf number to recompute, no quote to source-class. It either survives verbatim or the section schema-dies.

So the failure mode: Operator runs a real client they actually know. The numbers are all clean (your floor worked). But the *positioning thesis* — the load-bearing judgment they're going to put ad budget behind — is subtly wrong in a way only a domain expert catches. The buyer-ICP says the champion is the CFO when this operator knows for their client it's the FP&A manager. It reads authoritative. It's sourced (the agentic writer cited a real but misread case study). The operator's reaction is not "one bad cell, strip it" — it's **"if it's confidently wrong about the thing I happen to know, what about the six sections I *don't* know?"** Trust collapses globally from one local qualitative miss, because there's no visible uncertainty signal distinguishing a grounded claim from a confident hallucination at the *sentence* level.

This is worse than the number problem because (a) it's invisible to your whole gate stack, (b) it's the highest-value, highest-attention part of the deck, and (c) the agentic writer's *strength* (fluent synthesis) is precisely what makes a wrong synthesis dangerous. Your value-read proof measured whether the prose is *good*, not whether the prose is *true to this subject*. An 8/8 on a value rubric and a wrong-about-the-client thesis are fully compatible. The plan has no mechanism — not even a manual one — to surface "which sentences are inference vs. which are grounded" to the operator. That's the gap that kills the pilot.

## B. Sequencing critique

**The sequencing is materially wrong in two places, and the strangler is over-engineered for this goal.**

**1. P3's cross-subject validation is catastrophically late — it must move to the front.** You have ONE data point: GLM scores 8/8 on TWO sections of ONE subject (Ramp). Everything downstream — P0's freeze, P1's contract lock, P2's entire spine rebuild and run-section strangle — is being committed *before* you know whether the agentic writer even generalizes. Ramp is the most-documented fintech on the internet; it's the easiest possible subject. The likely truth is that the 8/8 is partly a *Ramp corpus richness* artifact, not a *GLM capability* artifact. **You are about to rebuild your core engine on a sample size of one company and two of seven sections.** If GLM scores 4/8 on a data-sparse non-Ramp subject (the exact case where the corpus is thin and the writer must lean on tools that return little), the whole architecture target is invalidated *after* you've burned P0–P2. Move "prove GLM on 2-3 diverse non-Ramp subjects across all 7 sections" to immediately after P1 parity, *before* the spine rebuild. This is the cheapest possible de-risking and it gates everything.

**2. P2's strangle should FOLLOW cross-subject proof, not precede it.** The strangle of a 14,971-line file is the single most expensive, riskiest item in the plan. Doing it before you've confirmed the writer generalizes means you might be carefully strangling toward an architecture you're going to throw away. Order: prove writer cross-subject → *then* decide what the spine even needs to be → then strangle.

**3. The strangler-don't-swap parallel-diff harness is over-engineered for a 2-operator pilot.** Parallel-run-and-diff-every-commit-vs-absent-decision is a *production-migration-with-live-traffic* technique. You have two operators on supervised runs, not a million users you can't afford to regress. The cost of strangling-in-parallel (maintaining two code paths, building the diff harness, reconciling divergences) is enormous, and its benefit — catching silent regressions against the *old* path — assumes the old path is a trustworthy oracle. **It isn't.** The old DeepSeek path scores 1/8 and 3/8; diffing your new 8/8 path against a known-bad baseline tells you almost nothing useful ("it diverges" is the *goal*, not a regression signal). For two supervised operators, just **build the new spine, run both manually on the same 3 subjects, and have a human read both decks.** A human eyeballing 3 paired decks is cheaper, higher-signal, and catches the qualitative-trust problem (Part A) that an automated commit/absent diff never will. Cut the parallel-diff harness; keep a one-time manual side-by-side.

**4. P0's freeze is the right instinct but mis-sequenced relative to its own dependency** (the critic's circular-dependency point #1 is real). Given that, P0 as specced cannot be "green." Resolution: split P0. P0a = freeze the *fixtures and scorer* (cheap, no extraction needed). P0b = the post-strip-delta assertion moves *into* P2 alongside the strip extraction it depends on. Don't pretend P0 is a clean gate.

## C. The deepest tension — can a deterministic floor hold 8/8?

**No. A purely deterministic strip-or-gap floor cannot hold 8/8, and the proof is in your own evidence — but the resolution is NOT to make the floor agentic. It's to recognize you have two different jobs and stop conflating them.**

**The argument that deterministic suffices:** Determinism is the whole point of a *liar-catcher*. The moment your floor makes an LLM judgment, it can itself hallucinate — now you have a non-deterministic gate that can *introduce* fabrications while claiming to remove them, and you've lost the one property (replayability, auditability, "this exact input always strips to this exact output") that makes a floor trustworthy as a safety rail. An agentic floor is just a second writer; quis custodiet. For a trust pilot, a gate you can't reproduce is worse than no gate.

**The argument that it can't:** Your proven-8 was achieved by a repair that **recomputed and re-displayed a ratio** — an act of *judgment* (which numbers are the operands, what the correct ratio is, how to phrase it). A strip-or-gap floor by definition deletes; it never *produces* a corrected value. So the very transformation that earned the 8 is one a deterministic floor *structurally cannot perform*. If the floor only strips, the floor-capped output stays floor-capped (you lose the ratio entirely → the section drops below 8), or the writer's wrong ratio survives (→ fabrication). The 8 required *correction*, and correction is generation, not deletion. This is a genuine contradiction in the architecture target as stated: "deterministic floor that never auto-corrects" and "the 8 came from auto-correcting a ratio" cannot both be true of the same pipeline.

**Resolution — pick: deterministic floor, and move the correction UPSTREAM into the writer, not the floor.** The ratio recompute is not a *floor* job; it's a *writer competence* job. The agentic writer, which already has tool access to the raw payloads, should be instructed/structured to **derive ratios from its fetched leaf numerics and never assert a ratio it didn't compute** — making the ratio correct *by construction* at generation time. Then the deterministic floor's job shrinks to its true job: **verify** the displayed ratio matches a recompute from the cited operands, and **strip-or-gap** if it doesn't. Determinism is preserved (floor only checks + deletes), and the 8 is preserved (correction happens in the writer where judgment legitimately lives, then gets *verified* deterministically). The mistake in the plan is asking the floor to be both the corrector and the verifier. Split them: writer corrects, floor verifies-and-strips. **Do not make the floor agentic** — that destroys the only property worth having.

One caveat that strengthens this: a deterministic *verify-the-ratio-matches-operands* check is exactly the leaf-numeric-vs-tool-payload gap the critic flagged (#3). So this resolution also closes that hole — the floor recomputes from the *originating tool's returned payload*, not from the writer's restated operands. That's the single highest-value addition to the floor.

## D. Over-built vs. under-built for a 2-operator supervised pilot

**OVER-BUILT (cut to ship faster):**

1. **The parallel-strangle diff harness** (per Part B). Replace with a one-time manual paired-deck read on 3 subjects. Saves the most time of anything here.
2. **The SHA-256 byte-freeze "acknowledgement gate" on 12 verifier files.** This is process theater for a 2-person pilot. The risk it addresses (someone silently edits a verifier) doesn't exist when you're the one writing all the code over a few weeks. Freeze the *fixtures and the scorer* (those re-banding silently is real), but byte-freezing source files you're actively refactoring is friction with near-zero payoff at this scale. Cut it.
3. **Per-section trust *tiers* in P4.** A graduated tier UX is product-grade. For 2 operators, you need exactly one binary signal per section: "grounded vs. inferred" (or even just a confidence dot). Ship the crude version.
4. **Extending the full thin-floor to all 5 remaining sections before the pilot** — but only conditionally (see under-built #3). Two of seven sections proved the thesis; you don't need bulletproof floors on all seven to start a *supervised* pilot where a human reads everything.

**UNDER-BUILT (will bite):**

1. **The qualitative-trust signal (Part A).** Nothing in the plan surfaces, even manually, which prose is grounded vs. inferred. This is the actual product for the operator and it has zero gate, zero UX, zero validation. *This is the biggest under-build.* Minimum viable: have the writer tag each load-bearing strategic claim with its grounding source (corpus span / tool result / "inference"), and render inference-claims visibly distinct. Operators can then trust-but-verify the inferences and ignore the grounded ones. Without this, Part A kills the pilot.

2. **Operator workflow when a section is wrong.** The plan covers "don't let rerun silently destroy a committed section" (good) but not "operator sees a wrong claim — now what?" Can they correct it? Override it? Flag it back into the corpus? A supervised pilot's *entire value* is the human-in-the-loop correcting and learning. With no edit/override/annotate path, operators read a wrong deck and... do nothing? The feedback loop that makes a supervised pilot worth running is absent.

3. **Per-section validation for BuyerICP (persona-grounding prepass) and PaidMedia (synthesis).** The critic flagged these; they're genuinely under-built because they're *different kinds* of sections. PaidMedia synthesizes the other six — if any upstream section is wrong, PaidMedia launders the error into a *budget recommendation the operator acts on with real money*. That's the highest-stakes section and it has no error-propagation guard. BuyerICP's persona-grounding (the recurring "0 personas committed" failure in your own memory) is a known live failure that the plan assumes a prepass fixes without proving it.

**Leanest critical path to "operators trust the deck on 3 real subjects":**

1. **z.ai parity** — prove 8/8 survives the endpoint/model-id swap (1 live action, gates everything). *[critic #4, agreed, first]*
2. **Cross-subject generalization** — run the agentic writer on 3 diverse non-Ramp subjects across all 7 sections, value-read each. This is the go/no-go for the entire architecture. *[currently buried in P3 — pull to the front]*
3. **Writer-corrects + deterministic-verify-and-strip floor** — ratio derived-by-construction in writer, leaf-numerics verified against originating tool payload in floor. *[Part C resolution + critic #3]*
4. **Grounding tags + binary grounded/inferred render** — the qualitative-trust signal. *[Part A — the missing product]*
5. **Operator override/annotate path + rerun-doesn't-destroy.** *[the human-in-loop value]*
6. **Manual paired-deck read on the 3 subjects → operators sign off.**

Notably absent from the lean path: the byte-freeze, the parallel-diff strangler, the trust *tiers*, and the full 14,971-line strangle (do that *after* the pilot proves the architecture is worth keeping — strangling toward a validated target is cheap; strangling toward an unvalidated one is the most expensive mistake on the board).

## E. Blind spots in the whole framing

**1. "Agentic writer + one thin floor" optimizes the wrong layer.** Your own value-read proof showed the win came from *generation* (8 vs 1), not from gating. Yet the plan spends ~3 of 5 phases on the floor/spine/strangle (the gate-and-plumbing layer) and treats the generalization of the *generator* as a late checkbox in P3. You proved generation is the lever, then wrote a plan that's 60% about the rail. The framing inverts your own evidence. The architecture should be "**validated agentic writer**, then a thin floor" — with the overwhelming majority of de-risking effort on *whether the writer generalizes*, not on the floor's machinery.

**2. The frozen corpus is the silent single point of failure, and it's outside the entire gate stack.** Every section consumes the frozen research corpus. If the corpus is stale, thin, or wrong about the subject (which is *guaranteed* to vary wildly across non-Ramp subjects), the agentic writer produces confident garbage from bad inputs and the floor passes it (sourced to the corpus = "grounded"). Garbage-in is invisible to a floor that trusts the corpus as ground truth. The plan never validates corpus *quality per subject*. On a data-sparse client — exactly your pilot's real-world case — this dominates everything. The floor guards against the writer lying; nothing guards against the *corpus* being wrong.

**3. The value-read rubric is being used as if it measures truth, when it measures quality.** (Reinforces Part A.) An 8/8 means "reads like valuable strategy," not "correct for this company." You're about to ship operator trust on a metric that doesn't measure the thing operators care about. You need a second axis — *accuracy/groundedness* — that you have not defined, frozen, or measured. The whole proof rests on one rubric optimizing for the wrong target.

**4. Is the floor even the right shape vs. alternatives?** Two alternatives the framing never considers: (a) **Generate-N-and-cross-check** — run the agentic writer 2-3× and flag claims that *don't survive across runs* as low-confidence (cheap consensus-as-confidence, catches hallucinations the floor can't, directly produces the grounded/inferred signal from Part A). (b) **Claim-level provenance as the primary architecture** rather than post-hoc stripping — make the writer emit every claim with a source pointer *by construction*, and render unsourced claims as inference. Both attack the qualitative-trust problem the strip-floor structurally can't touch. The plan locked "agentic writer + thin floor" before considering that the highest-value failure mode (confident wrong synthesis) needs a *different* defense than a number-stripping floor.

**5. Model lock-in risk under-weighed.** You've LOCKED on GLM-5.2 via a provider (z.ai/BigModel) on the strength of one subject. GLM is the *generation* lever — the most important component — and you're betting the architecture on a single model from a single provider whose endpoint/availability/pricing you don't yet control in prod. The DeepSeek "rollback" is correctly flagged (critic #5) as availability-not-quality, which means you have **no quality fallback at all** if GLM regresses or the provider has issues mid-pilot. For the one component that matters most, single-sourcing it on n=1 evidence is the framing's quietest large bet.

**Bottom line:** The plan is well-engineered for the wrong risk. It hardens the rail while the engine is proven on one easy subject and two of seven sections, and it has no defense — gate *or* UX — for the failure mode (confident, well-sourced, wrong *qualitative* strategy) that is both the deck's highest-value content and the most likely thing to break operator trust. Re-order to prove the generator generalizes *first*, split correction (writer) from verification (deterministic floor), add a grounded-vs-inferred signal, and cut the production-grade migration machinery that a 2-operator supervised pilot doesn't need.
